complicity-&-historical-hindsight.html.title: Complicity & Historical Hindsight
complicity-&-historical-hindsight.html.description: One of the novel's main moral messages is that good faith and sincerity is no defence for actions when the cause is wrong. Ono acted sincerely and believed his nationalism and propaganda served Japan nobly, however the cause is revealed to be catastrophic. The theme asks the audience, whether sincerity like the one Ono showed is a valid excuse, or whether an artist bears responsibility for the uses of his work regardless if his intent was sincere or 'evil'.
complicity-&-historical-hindsight.html.author: Samuel Wang
complicity-&-historical-hindsight.html.sourcePath: School/English/An Artist of the Floating World/Complicity & Historical Hindsight.md

concept-map.html.title: Concept Map
concept-map.html.description:
Ishiguro's use of a translation register allows for the formality of Japanese language to exhibit through Ono's narration even in English.
Ono recounts his past events through social language, and Japanese social language is full of hedging, deference, and circumlocution, and it is one of the most well-documented features of Japanese sociolinguistics.
Ishiguro is able to bake these aspects of language into Ono's english narration. When Ono says things such as "it is possible that..." or "I have reason to believe that on that occasion" or softening every claim with qualifiers, his narration appears natural culturally. This also means that Ono does not ever have to make a direct statement about his past actions, as the conventions of politeness weaved within the language allows Ono to hide between the lines of cultural formality and evasion, producing ambiguity and drives the novel's unreliable narration.
Ono narrates negative events, such as his role in the arrest of Kuroda, watching the collapse of his wartime influence, and seeing his daughter's marriage prospects and lives damaged by his reputation. However, he narrates these events in the same formal voice he uses to recount positive events, such as an evening at a bar with his students and colleagues. The flatness and absence of emotion is a form of unreliable narration as Ono's voice refuses to acknowledge the emotion that should show in the language when recounting some of the more traumatic memories, suppressing their emotional weight. How Ono's translation register reveals a layer of unreliable narration
Source: https://www.theguardian.com/books/2016/jun/24/kazuo-ishiguro-my-turning-point-reading-proust-on-my-sickbed
Ishiguro states that he invented a voice for Ono's narration that reads as if the English that is standing in and morphing into Japanese formalities. Ishiguro says, "... an elegant yet slightly stilted register that would suggest the rhythms and stylised formality of the Japanese language..." The Japanese language is often considered extremely formal, and Ishiguro wanted the novel to be understood in Japanese, though written in English, as if the book was a translation, and all the english sentences had Japanese ones behind them, even stating "This strategy had implications for every word I wrote on the page." Ishiguro wanted the language to "flow and to feel natural" without being too colloquial, or too "English"Ishiguro's Construct of a "Translation" register
Source: https://www.theguardian.com/books/2016/jun/24/kazuo-ishiguro-my-turning-point-reading-proust-on-my-sickbed
In the article, Ishiguro names his own personal fear, “And for me personally: the nagging sense of how difficult it is to see clearly above the dogmatic fervours of one’s day; and the fear that time and history would show that for all one’s good intentions, one had backed a wrong, shameful, even evil cause, and wasted one’s best years and talents to it.”
This statement is a close description of Ono's own life. Ono devoted his best years and his real talent as an artist to a cause that he believed was right and noble, which was his nationalist projects. However, history reveals it as shameful. Through a projection of Ono, Ishiguro is confessing that Ono's issues is his own fear, and Ono represents a construct that has been built as if his fear had already come true. This tells the audience that Ishiguro did not write about Ono for Ono to be condemned and shamed, he wrote him with a fear and sympathy, as Ono is who Ishiguro was afraid of becoming, and this reveals the purpose of the unreliable narration and self deception of Ono.Rather than unreliable narration and self deception being a process of villainy and deception, it is simply a human process of a person unable to face the thought that they gave their life to something that was now deemed shameful, and the evasions, drifting memories, and reframing are like psychological coping mechanisms of someone who's entire self esteem and sense of self worth depends on NOT seeing themselves clearly. The unreliability becomes tragic rather than deceptive. Instead of watching a liar attempting to hide his actions, the narrative presents a story of a man's mind protecting him from a truth destroy him. The audience is able to see what Ono's mind is rejecting and cannot bear seeing, resulting in the audience feeling pity, not satisfaction in Ono's sufferings.Ono is essentially a mirror to the audience about the fears that Ishiguro puts forward and makes the audience think, how would you know, in your own political moment and thoughts, whether or not you are an 'Ono', and how is anyone able to see clearly, as Ishiguro puts it "above the dogmatic fervours of one’s day".Ishiguro's personal fear
Ono's narrative structure differs from conventionally structured novels, where a narrator could tell the audience of their self-deception, explicitly depicting their denial. Ono utilises his inspiration of Proust's work and makes the structure of narration itself reveal the deception.Whenever Ono remembers something painful or negative in his past, such as his role in Kuroda's arrest, his responsibility for the failed marriage negotiations etc, the memory always shifts to a different time period with a different event. Ono is not denying the truth or refusing to talk about the event, he is coincidently just remembering something else. The structural movement mimics psychological avoidance, which is when the mind uses protective behaviours to redirect itself before it faces its fears. It provides immediate emotional relief, but in the long term it reinforces fears and maintains anxiety disorders.
Ishiguro also places scenes next to one another and lets the audience map out the relationship between them, as stated in How the structure of Ono's narration betrays his story. The disarray between what the structure is telling the audience and what the narrator claims reveals the self-deception of Ono.How the structure of Ono's narration enacts self-deception
In An Artist of the Floating World, Ishiguro writes in a non-linear narration structure through writing scenes in order of association (See The Proustian influence on the narrative structure of "An Artist of the Floating World").
In the October 1948 section where Ono is narrating the event of discussions around Noriko's upcoming miai with the Saito family and about his unease involving the previous failed negotiations with the Miyake family. Mid narration, his flow of memory turns the event being narrated into his days studying under Mori-san, his break from his sensei's aestheticism and being An Artist of the Floating World, his turn towards propaganda art, and his rising influence in wartime cultural circles.Ono presents these as fond memories, such as "let me tell you about my career." However, it makes the reader ponder about why Ono, who was in the middle of worrying about his daughter's marriage prospects, is now recounting the story of his former wartime glory to the audience. This structure reveals how Ono's flow of memory is betraying him to the reader. Although Ono explicitly states that the Miyake's family withdrawal from marriage negotiations was unrelated to his reputation during the war, his 'flow of memory' style of narration reveals how the two memories are connected, and hence the audience is able to see that the Miyake's negotiations fell apart due to Ono's wartime reputation. Ono never states this explicitly but the proximity of his recounts events exposes the truth.How the structure of Ono's narration betrays his storyThrough trapping the reader inside a singular consciousness with no objective image, Ishiguro forces the audience to experience the self-deception, not just observe it, as we are denied an outside view, and can only feel the claustrophobia of a mind that betrays and cannot escape itself. The effect of this on the audience is the intimacy of this style of narration transforming into an unease as we realise Ono's voice is concealing the truth.Quote: “I find it hard to understand how any man who values his self-respect would wish for long to avoid responsibility for his past deeds.” Ono states this while he spends the entire novel avoiding responsibility for his past actions. However, because of the nature of the interiority narration, the audience cannot see the events that Ono is dodging, only the gap between his statements and actual evasion. A film, which what Ishiguro rejects, would show the reader the contradiction, while prose makes us infer it, implicating the reader more.Quote: “I do not wish to appear complacent, but… I am not one of those who are afraid to admit to the shortcomings of past achievements.” "I do not wish to appear" is an example of common Japanese deflection, allowing Ono to raise his own virtues and morals while pretending to disclaim his evasion. The formality of the language sounds humble to others while it functions as self-praise.Ono's other habitual language patterns such as “I believe,” “it is possible,” “I have a feeling I may have said something like…,” “or so it seemed to me”, shows how he never commits to a plain factual claim regarding his past, and the politeness conventions make the evasions plausible and appear to be normal.Summary: Ono's non-linear narration does not contain a narrative about a man in self-deception like conventional stories, the structure itself reveals a layer of self-deception through ordering events by association of memory, and inviting the audience to infer the relationship between events based on their proximity within the narrative. Ishiguro's rejection of "prose screenplay" provides the foundation of unreliable narration through the interiority which allows the unreliable narration to continue by denying the audience an objective reality, rather the only reality is Ono's construct.
Source: https://www.theguardian.com/books/2016/jun/24/kazuo-ishiguro-my-turning-point-reading-proust-on-my-sickbed
Ishiguro credits reading the "Overture" and "Combray" sections of Proust's Remembrance of Things for non-linear narration and his novel's form.
Ishiguro adopted from Proust what he called "methods of movement", where scenes are ordered not by chronology or linear plot, but by thought association and the vagaries of memory (unpredictable, erratic and uncontrollable nature of human recollection). This is why Ono's narration is non-linear, where a scene from two days ago is placed next to one from twenty years ago, resulting in the reader being asked to infer the relationship.
An example of this is in the October 1948 section where Ono is narrating the event of discussions around Noriko's upcoming miai with the Saito family and about his unease involving the previous failed negotiations with the Miyake family. Mid narration, his flow of memory turns the event being narrated into his days studying under Mori-san, his break from his sensei's aestheticism and being An Artist of the Floating World, his turn towards propaganda art, and his rising influence in wartime cultural circles.The Proustian influence on the narrative structure of "An Artist of the Floating World"The polite voice coaxes the reader into trusting what Ono is stating, as he sounds humble and reasonable like a reliable narrator, which is the trap. The audience gradually learns to read against the translational register instead of believing it, and treating the standard politeness as a warning sign. In a way, the novel educates the audience that not all unreliability sounds like lying, but can sound like genuine and simple courtesy instead.Quote: “But then again, it is perhaps in the nature of things that a man will not know the full consequences of his own deeds until it is too late.” Ono removes the personalisation of his problem and wants to generalise his own guilts into something like a universal law of human nature through use of language such as "a man" and "the nature of things". This movement itself is self-deception, and by portraying his failure as inevitable fate, he blurs his particular responsibility. It also uses low modality language such as "perhaps" and "it is in the nature of things" which enforces his attempt to cover up his actions. The second sentence also reveals a deep hypocrisy, and despite Ono judging those who are unable or unwilling to acknowledge one's mistakes, his entire narration is doing that. Ono names the standard by which he should be judged himself and states it loud and clear without seeing how it perfectly fits himself, showing his lack of self awareness.Quote: "In any case, there is surely no great shame in mistakes made in the best of faith. It is surely a thing far more shameful to be unable or unwilling to acknowledge them." This quote is an example of a recurring sentence, and each time Ono uses, it reveals more anxiety than reassurance, as he is restating and rehearsing his own defences, the repetition showing he is struggling to move on and still clinging onto his self worth.The non-linear narration pushes the audience into a position of one similar to a detective and judge. Due to Ono not connecting the events on his own, the reader is forced to, and through reassembling the timeline of events, the guilt of the narrator feels discovered rather than straight up told to the audience. This produces a discomfort where, because the audience knows more than Ono admits to knowing, even in the interiority of the narration style, we as the readers are given the power to do the accusing and judging that Ono will not, and as a result, feel uneasy about how readily we should judge a man who is clearly already suffering. Since Ono also states the standard himself clearly, but we cannot dismiss him as stupid or evil for that, making the pity and sympathy stronger.Across the whole narrative, Ono returns to a few memories many times, such as the confrontation with Kuroda and the nature of his influence, and he does this across multiple sections of the novel. Each retelling of the event changes slightly, an example being:This mimics how self-deception works. Every time a story that contains lies is revisited, it is adjusted a little to appear more and more real as the person wishes to maintain the story. The non-linear structure allows for the revisiting of his events and his process of revising and changing events to be visible to the audience and revealing hidden layers of self-deception, while it remains unconscious and invisible to Ono.Ono's narration by association shows the connections between events that he unconsciously makes through Ishiguro's chosen narrative structure, even when his conscious mind denies it.How Ono's repetition of events reveals self-deceptionWhen knowing that the narrator embodies the author's own fear changes the lens of the audience into one of sympathy and tragedy rather than judgement. The audience is guided towards a feeling of moral superiority compared to Ono, but then towards pity and finally to self comparison and judgement about whether they are able to apply this fear to themselves. In the end, through making the self deception feel human and close, the reader leaves not angry or judgemental of Ono, but extremely frightened of their own capacity to become him.Quote: “... the fear that time and history would show that for all one’s good intentions, one had backed a wrong, shameful, even evil cause, and wasted one’s best years and talents to it.” - Ishiguro “... there is surely no great shame in mistakes made in the best of faith.” - Ono When set by side, the author's fear and the character's self comfort and deception stem from the same thought, only at different stages. Ishiguro greatly fears, while Ono is living inside of the fear, only surviving through his unescapable self deception, revealing how Ono is written through pity and sympathy by Ishiguro.
Source: https://www.theguardian.com/books/2016/jun/24/kazuo-ishiguro-my-turning-point-reading-proust-on-my-sickbed
Ishiguro was reading his own first novel next to the screenplays he was writing for Channel 4, which resulted in "a significant, if contrary impact of the writing of An Artist". Ishiguro noticed his fiction looked like a line of dialogue from the screenplays, which was a bit of description of what someone did, followed by lines of dialogue. Ishiguro referred to it as "prose screenplay", his fear being that if a novel is just describing observable events and dialogue, then it is not offering anything more than what you could get from watching a TV show, so as Ishiguro states, "Why bother to write a novel if it was going to offer more or less the same experience someone could have by turning on a television?"
Ishiguro solves this through the use of interiority, or living inside a mind (Ono's) with all its distortions about the truth and lies. If An Artist of the Floating World was to be filmed, a camera would reveal and show Ono reporting his student, Kuroda, to the authorities, and the audience would be able to see the consequences on Kuroda's face. They would have the objective truth about the event, and Ono's attempts to minimise and redirect away from it would be easily exposed as lies.However, Ishiguro's rejection of "prose screenplay" resulted in an interiority where there is no resulting objective footage and only Ono's unreliable accounts. Ishiguro locks the audience inside a single mind with no way to verify its thoughts against reality, which is only possible in prose. By writing against screenplay, Ishiguro creates a story where the only reality is Ono's own constructs. Without this structural choice, the unreliability of Ono fails as the readers can see through an objective lense.Rejection of "Prose Screenplay"By addressing a primarily British anxiety through the farce of historical Japan, Ishiguro lowers the guard of the audience, as it is a story about someone else, a different war, but then Ishiguro uses the mirror 'Ono' to force a delayed and uncomfortable self application of the fears and issues faced by Ono, as well as the consequences of such principles. This results in the reader not finishing the book and judging Ono, but rather wondering whether or not they can recognise the same issues Ono had inside them, as Ono could not despite his talent.The Floating World allows the audience to understand Ono through setting before anything that he admits directly. The motif of transience among both the Floating World and Ono creates a dramatic irony where we see everything that Ono values as already doomed, and it results in the audience reading his pride and denial as poignant rather than persuasive, again producing pity rather than contempt as the reader is pulled into the position of knowing more than the narrator, Ono.
Themes and symbols of An Artist of the Floating WorldThe Floating World (ukiyo) is a pre-war pleasure district depicted often in woodblock prints which show the pleasure seeking life of Edo period Japan of bars and nocturnal beauty. By definition it is transient, put together at night and vanish with the morning. The Floating World works as a symbol of Ono's transience as his prestige and moral certainties all vanished like the pleasure district itself, and as a tool for self deception as Ono mourns a vanished world, shown through his muses on rebuilding the pleasure district and reminiscing on his days in the bars with his colleagues and students, however Ono himself helped destroy this world without him seeing his involvement or connection.The Floating World (transcience & self-deception)Quote 2: "... the factories had yet to take the fragrance of seasonal blossoms from the night air." - Ono Ono here is grieving the industrial progress of Japan represented by the factories, erasing the beauty of seasonal blossoms from the night air, alluding to the pleasure district of a floating world. However, Ono aligns himself with these nationalist thoughts and forces of progress that destroyed the old world, and so Ono mourns a loss he helped cause, with total moral blindness about his contribution in the destruction, resulting in self deception being used at the level of nostalgia.Quote: "The best things, he always used to say, are put together of a night and vanish with the morning. What people call the floating world, Ono, was a world Gisaburo knew how to value." - Mori-san This quote directly correlates to the title's meaning of a Floating World, and it predicts Ono's fate, whose reputation and influence were also "put together of a night" in the form of the bars and nationalist gatherings of Migi-Hidari, which have also vanished. The impermanence of the Floating World mirrors the impermanence of everything Ono has managed to build.
Authorial and Historical Context of An Artist of the Floating WorldSummary:
Ishiguro's personal fears during the time of Thatcher's Britain in the 1980s shaped the way the audience views the self-deception and unreliable narration of Ono, not as lying but defence, and it makes the audience feel pity instead of satisfaction for Ono's suffering based on what he does. The reader also would see how easy and how human the process of self-deception is, and be frightened or have their current thoughts and political ideals potentially challenged by it.Exploring how Thatcher’s Britain is used as a real subject reveals the novel’s central question: what does an artist owe his society? Ishiguro’s fears during the 1980s, agonising about the role of the artist in a time of political change is projected onto Ono’s experiences in postwar Japan.Quote: “We at least acted on what we believed and did our utmost… we were not, at least, coward​s.” Here, Ono is clinging into sincerity as a defence, through language such as "we believed we weren't cowards". However, one of the novel's main questions are whether that is an excuse for a defence at all, when the cause was so catastrophic, reflecting Ishiguro's anxiety about the Thatcher era, about the pressure to take a side, and the fear that believe and conviction itself does not guarantee anything you put your life for to be right or just.
Source: https://www.theguardian.com/books/2016/jun/24/kazuo-ishiguro-my-turning-point-reading-proust-on-my-sickbed
Ishiguro wrote An Artist of the Floating World during a politically difficult time in Britain during the 1980s when it was the era of the miner's strike, the Wapping dispute, CND marches, the Falkland war, IRA terrorism, the economic theory of "Monetarism", etc. Ishiguro states he was living in a place where 'the pressures on people in every walk of life to take political sides; the rigid certainties, shading into self-righteousness and sinister aggression, of ardent, often youthful factions; the agonising about the “role of the artist” in a time of political change.'Ishiguro states that the novel was very much shaped by the Britain in which Ishiguro was in, and the pressures on the people living in Britain to take political sides translates directly onto Ono in the context of wartime Japan.
Ono succumbed to the pressure to take a political side by leaving behind the apolitical aestheticism of the floating world of pleasure and beauty of his teacher Mori-san in order to make nationalist propaganda art, solidifying that he had picked a side. Ono did not only hold nationalist views, he also enforced them, shown by when he reported his own student, Kuroda to the authorities for being unpatriotic, which resulted in the arrest and torturing of Kuroda, revealing the 'sinister aggression of ardent factions' and making it personal and up close.By setting it in postwar Japan, Ishiguro can explore his own anxieties in 1980s about Britain at a safe distance, he is not writing about Thatcher, but using an already passed historical event, being the militarism of Japan and its defeat, in order to think about what it feels like to be inside a political moment, where you also cannot see who is right achieved through the interiority of Ono. Thatcher's Britain as a real subject
Structure of Ono's Narration
Voice of Ono's NarrationsSummary: Ishiguro's construct of Ono's translation register within his narration allows for the conventional formalities and Japanese sociolinguistics to exhibit their properties through the English, allowing Ono to hide behind cultural formalities instead of presenting his voice as one of personal evasion.
The novel's central question which is, what does an artist owe his society, and whether it is more beneficial and nobler to stay and retreat into beauty like the floating world and Mori-san or to engage politically, which was Ono's choice. And what are the consequences and effect when the political cause that you served turns out to be catastrophic (shown by the damage Ono's reputation causes, and his unwillingness to move on).Ono believed that an artist must serve a greater cause than just beauty to its society, while Mori-san represents the opposite, being art for the sake of beauty and the apolitical floating world. The novel questions both sides, whether or not it is more noble or naive to stay in the safety of beauty or whether or not it is more noble or dangerous to make art politically involved, and it also leaves the question of, since Ono's circumstances was such a tragedy, is the artist truly responsible for what their art is used for?The role of the artist in political changeQuote: “... the finest, most fragile beauty an artist can hope to capture drifts within those pleasure houses after dark.” - (Mori-san’s philosophy, paraphrased in Ono’s recollection) "Artists must learn to value something more tangible than those pleasurable things that disappear with the morning light. It is not necessary that artists always occupy a decadent and enclosed world. - Ono Mori-san's art values the fragile and drifting and transient beauty that admits its own impermanence. Ono wished to produce something more significant, and sought permanence in comparison to Mori-san. However, the novel builds the irony of Ono's goal as his propaganda works appeared to be more shameful and transient than the fragile beauty of Mori-san's art that Ono rejected.Ishiguro forces the reader to consider the question of which side to pick rather than just giving an answer of beauty vs purpose, through the display of Ono's failed choice. Ono's reasoning makes sense, in that art should matter and should engage and have a purpose rather than just look good. However when this seemingly responsible and correct choice leads to the tragedy of Ono's events, while the 'meaningless' path stayed innocent and without accusation. This forces an uncomfortable realisation onto the audience, that even when chasing something sincerely and that choice seemingly obvious to be correct, it can result in disaster, while the alternative is to spend your life doing meaningless things.One of the novel's main moral messages is that good faith and sincerity is no defence for actions when the cause is wrong. Ono acted sincerely and believed his nationalism and propaganda served Japan nobly, however the cause is revealed to be catastrophic. The theme asks the audience, whether sincerity like the one Ono showed is a valid excuse, or whether an artist bears responsibility for the uses of his work regardless if his intent was sincere or 'evil'. Complicity & Historical HindsightMemory in the novel is active, serving the story and revised over time. The unreliability of memory is what the novel is about, and Ono's repeated admittance that his own memories may be incorrect reveal the theme surfacing.Memory as UnreliableThe novel is staged in a nation that is reforming itself from the old order of Bushido, Edo-era honour codes and imperial nationalism to a westernised, democratic, commercial postwar Japan, and this embodied in the two generations in the novel. His daughters, Setsuko and Noriko and his grandson represent the new Japan while Ono is still clinging onto the old world and values.An example is Ichiro, Ono's grandson, idolising cowboys over samurai heroes, where Ichiro's imagination has already been colonised by the West, and Ono's discomfort shows that he clings to the idea that Japanese heroes should inspire the boy with the same train of thought that once drove the motivation behind his nationalist art, revealing he has not yet moved on. This small detail also shows the entire extent of the paradigm shift, how the next generation won't even inherit the values Ono gave his entire life to. The audience sees the old world ending through a child's harmless playing with his cowboy western idol, which makes it feel inevitable rather than a contested possibility.Another example is Setsuko's repeated warnings to Ono about the marriage negotiations and how he may need to take "precautionary steps". Setsuko does not attack Ono's reputation directly as the new world is too polite for that, however she pressures him to disown it for their family's survival. This interaction shows the generational change, where the child now instructs the parents and the values that Ono once honoured are now a liability, revealing how the postwar order totally changed and overturned the old hierarchy.Ishiguro asks the audience a question through the use of this theme which is whether old values were noble or shameful, and whether the young ones are truly liberated, or erasing history simply because it was inconvenient. The audience is swayed both ways, with relief that the old militarist order is gone, but with unease at how easily the new generation discards valuable history, and how Ono is discredited as a result and this generates pity. The effect of this is a challenge of the conventional, easily progressing narrative, as neither one side is determined to be correct to condemn or celebrate. The new world is freer but colder, and the audience is unsure whether Ono's situation is justice or just erasure of history in ignorance.Generational change & the East-West paradigm shiftThe use of framing as ordinary men tempts the reader to accept it as the truth, as it is sympathetic towards Ono and also true that most people lack "special gifts of insight" and Ono's choice would have also appeared to be responsible and noble at times without having known the future. However, the novel's events also push the other way, as being ordinary didn't stop Ono from reporting Kuroda and causing real damage, resulting in the reader feeling an uncomfortable contradiction from the pull of the excuse and the excuses' ineffectiveness. In addition, since the quote is spoken by another character and only absorbed by Ono, the audience is watching self-deception happen socially, not just in oneself, where there are two men helping each other to lie and not look, which is more unsettling to the audience than a singular narrator lying to himself, and reveals its deep roots even in society. 'But there's no need to blame ourselves unduly,' he said. 'We at least acted on what we believed and did our utmost. It's just that in the end we turned out to be ordinary men. Ordinary men with no special gifts of insight. It was simply our misfortune to have been ordinary men during such times.' - Ono recalling Matsuda's statements Here the defence is seen in the phrase "ordinary men", reframing the issue as inadequacy rather than guilt, essentially saying that Matsuda and Ono did nothing wrong, the simply lacked "special gifts of insight". The repetition of "ordinary men" works like a comforting phrase that is repeated for their self worth rather than a conclusion being reached. "Misfortune" is a major evasion as well, framing their bad morals as bad luck instead, as if being complicit in a horrible cause was something that just happened to them, rather than something they chose, changing them into 'victims of the times'.Ono's own admission of his skewed memory trains the reader to read everything with a certain caution, but also creates a strange intimacy as he seems humble and candid at first, and the effect is uncomfortable and destabilising. The audience can never settle on the real events of what really happened because of this, so Ishiguro forces the audience to focus instead on WHY Ono remembers the events this way. This shifts attention from the event to the psychology behind behind it.

Each retelling of the event changes slightly, an example being:This mimics how self-deception works. Every time a story that contains lies is revisited, it is adjusted a little to appear more and more real as the person wishes to maintain the story. The non-linear structure allows for the revisiting of his events and his process of revising and changing events to be visible to the audience and revealing hidden layers of self-deception, while it remains unconscious and invisible to Ono.Ono's narration by association shows the connections between events that he unconsciously makes through Ishiguro's chosen narrative structure, even when his conscious mind denies it.

how-ono's-translation-register-reveals-a.title: How Ono's translation register reveals a layer of unreliable narration
how-ono's-translation-register-reveals-a.description:
Ishiguro's use of a translation register allows for the formality of Japanese language to exhibit through Ono's narration even in English.
Ono recounts his past events through social language, and Japanese social language is full of hedging, deference, and circumlocution, and it is one of the most well-documented features of Japanese sociolinguistics.
Ishiguro is able to bake these aspects of language into Ono's english narration. When Ono says things such as "it is possible that..." or "I have reason to believe that on that occasion" or softening every claim with qualifiers, his narration appears natural culturally. This also means that Ono does not ever have to make a direct statement about his past actions, as the conventions of politeness weaved within the language allows Ono to hide between the lines of cultural formality and evasion, producing ambiguity and drives the novel's unreliable narration.
Ono narrates negative events, such as his role in the arrest of Kuroda, watching the collapse of his wartime influence, and seeing his daughter's marriage prospects and lives damaged by his reputation. However, he narrates these events in the same formal voice he uses to recount positive events, such as an evening at a bar with his students and colleagues. The flatness and absence of emotion is a form of unreliable narration as Ono's voice refuses to acknowledge the emotion that should show in the language when recounting some of the more traumatic memories, suppressing their emotional weight.
how-ono's-translation-register-reveals-a.author: Samuel Wang
how-ono's-translation-register-reveals-a.sourcePath: School/English/An Artist of the Floating World/How Ono's translation register reveals a layer of unreliable narration.md

how-the-structure-of-ono's-narration-bet.title: How the structure of Ono's narration betrays his story
how-the-structure-of-ono's-narration-bet.description:
In An Artist of the Floating World, Ishiguro writes in a non-linear narration structure through writing scenes in order of association (See The Proustian influence on the narrative structure of "An Artist of the Floating World").
In the October 1948 section where Ono is narrating the event of discussions around Noriko's upcoming miai with the Saito family and about his unease involving the previous failed negotiations with the Miyake family. Mid narration, his flow of memory turns the event being narrated into his days studying under Mori-san, his break from his sensei's aestheticism and being An Artist of the Floating World, his turn towards propaganda art, and his rising influence in wartime cultural circles.Ono presents these as fond memories, such as "let me tell you about my career." However, it makes the reader ponder about why Ono, who was in the middle of worrying about his daughter's marriage prospects, is now recounting the story of his former wartime glory to the audience. This structure reveals how Ono's flow of memory is betraying him to the reader. Although Ono explicitly states that the Miyake's family withdrawal from marriage negotiations was unrelated to his reputation during the war, his 'flow of memory' style of narration reveals how the two memories are connected, and hence the audience is able to see that the Miyake's negotiations fell apart due to Ono's wartime reputation. Ono never states this explicitly but the proximity of his recounts events exposes the truth.
how-the-structure-of-ono's-narration-bet.author: Samuel Wang
how-the-structure-of-ono's-narration-bet.sourcePath: School/English/An Artist of the Floating World/How the structure of Ono's narration betrays his story.md

how-the-structure-of-ono's-narration-ena.title: How the structure of Ono's narration enacts self-deception
how-the-structure-of-ono's-narration-ena.description:
Ono's narrative structure differs from conventionally structured novels, where a narrator could tell the audience of their self-deception, explicitly depicting their denial. Ono utilises his inspiration of Proust's work and makes the structure of narration itself reveal the deception.Whenever Ono remembers something painful or negative in his past, such as his role in Kuroda's arrest, his responsibility for the failed marriage negotiations etc, the memory always shifts to a different time period with a different event. Ono is not denying the truth or refusing to talk about the event, he is coincidently just remembering something else. The structural movement mimics psychological avoidance, which is when the mind uses protective behaviours to redirect itself before it faces its fears. It provides immediate emotional relief, but in the long term it reinforces fears and maintains anxiety disorders.
Ishiguro also places scenes next to one another and lets the audience map out the relationship between them, as stated in How the structure of Ono's narration betrays his story. The disarray between what the structure is telling the audience and what the narrator claims reveals the self-deception of Ono.
how-the-structure-of-ono's-narration-ena.author: Samuel Wang
how-the-structure-of-ono's-narration-ena.sourcePath: School/English/An Artist of the Floating World/How the structure of Ono's narration enacts self-deception.md

ishiguro's-construct-of-a-\"translation\.title: Ishiguro's Construct of a "Translation" register
ishiguro's-construct-of-a-\"translation\.description:
Source: https://www.theguardian.com/books/2016/jun/24/kazuo-ishiguro-my-turning-point-reading-proust-on-my-sickbed
Ishiguro states that he invented a voice for Ono's narration that reads as if the English that is standing in and morphing into Japanese formalities. Ishiguro says, "... an elegant yet slightly stilted register that would suggest the rhythms and stylised formality of the Japanese language..." The Japanese language is often considered extremely formal, and Ishiguro wanted the novel to be understood in Japanese, though written in English, as if the book was a translation, and all the english sentences had Japanese ones behind them, even stating "This strategy had implications for every word I wrote on the page." Ishiguro wanted the language to "flow and to feel natural" without being too colloquial, or too "English"
ishiguro's-construct-of-a-\"translation\.author: Samuel Wang
ishiguro's-construct-of-a-\"translation\.sourcePath: School/English/An Artist of the Floating World/Ishiguro's Construct of a "Translation" register.md

ishiguro's-personal-fear.html.title: Ishiguro's personal fear
ishiguro's-personal-fear.html.description:
Source: https://www.theguardian.com/books/2016/jun/24/kazuo-ishiguro-my-turning-point-reading-proust-on-my-sickbed
In the article, Ishiguro names his own personal fear, “And for me personally: the nagging sense of how difficult it is to see clearly above the dogmatic fervours of one’s day; and the fear that time and history would show that for all one’s good intentions, one had backed a wrong, shameful, even evil cause, and wasted one’s best years and talents to it.”
This statement is a close description of Ono's own life. Ono devoted his best years and his real talent as an artist to a cause that he believed was right and noble, which was his nationalist projects. However, history reveals it as shameful. Through a projection of Ono, Ishiguro is confessing that Ono's issues is his own fear, and Ono represents a construct that has been built as if his fear had already come true. This tells the audience that Ishiguro did not write about Ono for Ono to be condemned and shamed, he wrote him with a fear and sympathy, as Ono is who Ishiguro was afraid of becoming, and this reveals the purpose of the unreliable narration and self deception of Ono.Rather than unreliable narration and self deception being a process of villainy and deception, it is simply a human process of a person unable to face the thought that they gave their life to something that was now deemed shameful, and the evasions, drifting memories, and reframing are like psychological coping mechanisms of someone who's entire self esteem and sense of self worth depends on NOT seeing themselves clearly. The unreliability becomes tragic rather than deceptive. Instead of watching a liar attempting to hide his actions, the narrative presents a story of a man's mind protecting him from a truth destroy him. The audience is able to see what Ono's mind is rejecting and cannot bear seeing, resulting in the audience feeling pity, not satisfaction in Ono's sufferings.Ono is essentially a mirror to the audience about the fears that Ishiguro puts forward and makes the audience think, how would you know, in your own political moment and thoughts, whether or not you are an 'Ono', and how is anyone able to see clearly, as Ishiguro puts it "above the dogmatic fervours of one’s day".
ishiguro's-personal-fear.html.author: Samuel Wang
ishiguro's-personal-fear.html.sourcePath: School/English/An Artist of the Floating World/Ishiguro's personal fear.md

rejection-of-\"prose-screenplay\".html.title: Rejection of "Prose Screenplay"
rejection-of-\"prose-screenplay\".html.description:
Source: https://www.theguardian.com/books/2016/jun/24/kazuo-ishiguro-my-turning-point-reading-proust-on-my-sickbed
Ishiguro was reading his own first novel next to the screenplays he was writing for Channel 4, which resulted in "a significant, if contrary impact of the writing of An Artist". Ishiguro noticed his fiction looked like a line of dialogue from the screenplays, which was a bit of description of what someone did, followed by lines of dialogue. Ishiguro referred to it as "prose screenplay", his fear being that if a novel is just describing observable events and dialogue, then it is not offering anything more than what you could get from watching a TV show, so as Ishiguro states, "Why bother to write a novel if it was going to offer more or less the same experience someone could have by turning on a television?"
Ishiguro solves this through the use of interiority, or living inside a mind (Ono's) with all its distortions about the truth and lies. If An Artist of the Floating World was to be filmed, a camera would reveal and show Ono reporting his student, Kuroda, to the authorities, and the audience would be able to see the consequences on Kuroda's face. They would have the objective truth about the event, and Ono's attempts to minimise and redirect away from it would be easily exposed as lies.However, Ishiguro's rejection of "prose screenplay" resulted in an interiority where there is no resulting objective footage and only Ono's unreliable accounts. Ishiguro locks the audience inside a single mind with no way to verify its thoughts against reality, which is only possible in prose. By writing against screenplay, Ishiguro creates a story where the only reality is Ono's own constructs. Without this structural choice, the unreliability of Ono fails as the readers can see through an objective lense.
rejection-of-\"prose-screenplay\".html.author: Samuel Wang
rejection-of-\"prose-screenplay\".html.sourcePath: School/English/An Artist of the Floating World/Rejection of "Prose Screenplay".md

thatcher's-britain-as-a-real-subject.htm.title: Thatcher's Britain as a real subject
thatcher's-britain-as-a-real-subject.htm.description:
Source: https://www.theguardian.com/books/2016/jun/24/kazuo-ishiguro-my-turning-point-reading-proust-on-my-sickbed
Ishiguro wrote An Artist of the Floating World during a politically difficult time in Britain during the 1980s when it was the era of the miner's strike, the Wapping dispute, CND marches, the Falkland war, IRA terrorism, the economic theory of "Monetarism", etc. Ishiguro states he was living in a place where 'the pressures on people in every walk of life to take political sides; the rigid certainties, shading into self-righteousness and sinister aggression, of ardent, often youthful factions; the agonising about the “role of the artist” in a time of political change.'Ishiguro states that the novel was very much shaped by the Britain in which Ishiguro was in, and the pressures on the people living in Britain to take political sides translates directly onto Ono in the context of wartime Japan.
Ono succumbed to the pressure to take a political side by leaving behind the apolitical aestheticism of the floating world of pleasure and beauty of his teacher Mori-san in order to make nationalist propaganda art, solidifying that he had picked a side. Ono did not only hold nationalist views, he also enforced them, shown by when he reported his own student, Kuroda to the authorities for being unpatriotic, which resulted in the arrest and torturing of Kuroda, revealing the 'sinister aggression of ardent factions' and making it personal and up close.By setting it in postwar Japan, Ishiguro can explore his own anxieties in 1980s about Britain at a safe distance, he is not writing about Thatcher, but using an already passed historical event, being the militarism of Japan and its defeat, in order to think about what it feels like to be inside a political moment, where you also cannot see who is right achieved through the interiority of Ono.
thatcher's-britain-as-a-real-subject.htm.author: Samuel Wang
thatcher's-britain-as-a-real-subject.htm.sourcePath: School/English/An Artist of the Floating World/Thatcher's Britain as a real subject.md

the-floating-world-(transcience-&-self-d.title: The Floating World (transcience & self-deception)
the-floating-world-(transcience-&-self-d.description: The Floating World (ukiyo) is a pre-war pleasure district depicted often in woodblock prints which show the pleasure seeking life of Edo period Japan of bars and nocturnal beauty. By definition it is transient, put together at night and vanish with the morning. The Floating World works as a symbol of Ono's transience as his prestige and moral certainties all vanished like the pleasure district itself, and as a tool for self deception as Ono mourns a vanished world, shown through his muses on rebuilding the pleasure district and reminiscing on his days in the bars with his colleagues and students, however Ono himself helped destroy this world without him seeing his involvement or connection.
the-floating-world-(transcience-&-self-d.author: Samuel Wang
the-floating-world-(transcience-&-self-d.sourcePath: School/English/An Artist of the Floating World/The Floating World (transcience & self-deception).md

the-proustian-influence-on-the-narrative.title: The Proustian influence on the narrative structure of "An Artist of the Floating World"
the-proustian-influence-on-the-narrative.description:
Source: https://www.theguardian.com/books/2016/jun/24/kazuo-ishiguro-my-turning-point-reading-proust-on-my-sickbed
Ishiguro credits reading the "Overture" and "Combray" sections of Proust's Remembrance of Things for non-linear narration and his novel's form.
Ishiguro adopted from Proust what he called "methods of movement", where scenes are ordered not by chronology or linear plot, but by thought association and the vagaries of memory (unpredictable, erratic and uncontrollable nature of human recollection). This is why Ono's narration is non-linear, where a scene from two days ago is placed next to one from twenty years ago, resulting in the reader being asked to infer the relationship.
An example of this is in the October 1948 section where Ono is narrating the event of discussions around Noriko's upcoming miai with the Saito family and about his unease involving the previous failed negotiations with the Miyake family. Mid narration, his flow of memory turns the event being narrated into his days studying under Mori-san, his break from his sensei's aestheticism and being An Artist of the Floating World, his turn towards propaganda art, and his rising influence in wartime cultural circles.
the-proustian-influence-on-the-narrative.author: Samuel Wang
the-proustian-influence-on-the-narrative.sourcePath: School/English/An Artist of the Floating World/The Proustian influence on the narrative structure of "An Artist of the Floating World".md

the-role-of-the-artist-in-political-chan.title: The role of the artist in political change
the-role-of-the-artist-in-political-chan.description: The novel's central question which is, what does an artist owe his society, and whether it is more beneficial and nobler to stay and retreat into beauty like the floating world and Mori-san or to engage politically, which was Ono's choice. And what are the consequences and effect when the political cause that you served turns out to be catastrophic (shown by the damage Ono's reputation causes, and his unwillingness to move on).Ono believed that an artist must serve a greater cause than just beauty to its society, while Mori-san represents the opposite, being art for the sake of beauty and the apolitical floating world. The novel questions both sides, whether or not it is more noble or naive to stay in the safety of beauty or whether or not it is more noble or dangerous to make art politically involved, and it also leaves the question of, since Ono's circumstances was such a tragedy, is the artist truly responsible for what their art is used for?
the-role-of-the-artist-in-political-chan.author: Samuel Wang
the-role-of-the-artist-in-political-chan.sourcePath: School/English/An Artist of the Floating World/The role of the artist in political change.md

info_showOrphanNodes.description: Show nodes that are not connected to any other nodes.

info_showAttachments.description: Show attachments like images and PDFs as nodes in the graph.

info_allowGlobalGraph.description: Allow the user to view the global graph of all nodes.

info_allowExpand.description: Allow the user to pop-out the graph view to take up the whole screen

info_attractionForce.description: How much should linked nodes attract each other? This will make the graph appear more clustered.

info_linkLength.description: How long should the links between nodes be? The shorter the links the more connected nodes will cluster together.

info_repulsionForce.description: How much should nodes repel each other? This will make disconnected parts more spread out.

info_centralForce.description: How much should nodes be attracted to the center? This will make the graph appear more dense and circular.

info_edgePruning.description: Edges with a length above this threshold will not be rendered, however they will still contribute to the simulation. This can help large tangled graphs look more organised. Hovering over a node will still display these links.

info_minNodeRadius.description: How small should the smallest nodes be? The smaller a node is the less it will attract other nodes.

info_maxNodeRadius.description: How large should the largest nodes be? Nodes are sized by how many links they have. The larger a node is the more it will attract other nodes. This can be used to create a good grouping around the most important nodes.

info_allowResizing.description: Whether or not to allow the sidebars to be resized

info_allowCollapsing.description: Whether or not to allow the sidebars to be collapsed

info_rightDefaultWidth.description: The default width of the right sidebar

info_leftDefaultWidth.description: The default width of the left sidebar
